ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general lobster food product class (FOODON_03411433); no Australia-specific lobster subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LN covers lobster generically; no Australia-specific rock lobster code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227261009 covers lobster as food generically; no species-specific Australian rock lobster code confirmed. Closest parent used.

main_ingredients: Australian rock lobster (Panulirus cygnus or Jasus edwardsii)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Australian lobster in Singapore is approximately 100 g of cooked lobster meat, often presented as half a whole lobster or as a plated portion.

nutrient_source.title: Lobster, cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15147)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked lobster (moist heat) used as the primary nutrient reference for Australian rock lobster (Panulirus / Jasus spp.). Australian rock lobster is nutritionally comparable to North Atlantic lobster (Homarus americanus) for macronutrient and micronutrient profiling. Values represent plain cooked lobster meat without added sauces or butter, consistent with the 100 g description in the input record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for Australian rock lobster exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. Lobster is a high-protein, very low-carbohydrate food (1.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g). By analogy with other cooked crustaceans and high-protein seafoods, GI is estimated at approximately 5 (effectively negligible), consistent with the principle that foods with negligible digestible carbohydrate have a GI near zero. GL per 100 g serving = (5 × 1.3) / 100 = 0.065, rounded to 0.1. This is consistent with the treatment of high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafoods in Atkinson et al. 2008.

hypertension.guidance: Australian lobster contains approximately 380 mg sodium per 100 g of cooked meat; individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size and avoid high-sodium preparations such as salted butter sauces or heavily seasoned broths, keeping total sodium intake within the recommended 1500–2000 mg/day limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and negligible glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 1.3 g of carbohydrate per 100 g and an estimated GI of approximately 5, Australian lobster has a negligible glycemic load and is a suitable high-protein, low-carbohydrate protein source for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided it is not served with high-sugar or high-starch sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content (145 mg per 100 g) offset by low saturated fat and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Australian lobster is relatively high in dietary cholesterol (approximately 145 mg per 100 g) but low in saturated fat (0.2 g); current cardiovascular guidelines focus more on saturated and trans fat than dietary cholesterol, and the presence of polyunsaturated fatty acids including omega-3s is beneficial, so moderate consumption as part of a heart-healthy diet is generally acceptable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Australian Lobster (澳洲龙虾, Àozhōu lóngxiā) refers to rock lobster species native to Australian waters, principally the Western Rock Lobster (Panulirus cygnus) and the Southern Rock Lobster (Jasus edwardsii). In Singapore's Chinese seafood restaurant scene, Australian lobster is a premium ingredient served at live seafood restaurants and is prized for its sweet, firm, and succulent white meat. It is commonly prepared by steaming with ginger and scallion, stir-frying with egg and butter, baking with cheese, or cooking in a superior stock broth. The flesh is dense and slightly chewy with a naturally sweet oceanic flavour. Nutritionally, Australian lobster is an excellent high-protein, low-fat food providing approximately 98 kcal, 20.5 g protein, and only 1.0 g fat per 100 g of cooked meat. It is a rich source of selenium (59 mcg), zinc (3.4 mg), vitamin B12 (1.4 mcg), and phosphorus (240 mg), making it nutritionally dense relative to its caloric content. The carbohydrate content is negligible at 1.3 g per 100 g, resulting in a very low glycemic load suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate at 380 mg per 100 g for plain cooked meat, though restaurant preparations with added sauces may significantly increase sodium. Cholesterol is relatively high at 145 mg per 100 g, though saturated fat is low. Australian lobster is halal and gluten-free in its natural form. It is a culturally significant luxury seafood in Singapore Chinese cuisine, often served at celebratory banquets and family gatherings.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Australian lobster?
Is Australian lobster high in cholesterol?
Best way to cook Australian lobster Singapore

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked lobster (moist heat), which is nutritionally comparable to Australian rock lobster species. No Australia-specific rock lobster entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. GI is an analogous estimate based on the negligible carbohydrate content of cooked lobster, consistent with peer-reviewed GI literature for high-protein low-carbohydrate seafoods. Confidence is medium rather than high because an exact species-specific Australian rock lobster entry from a primary regional source was not available.
notes: 1. Food name corrected from input typo 'Autralian Lobster' to canonical 'Australian Lobster'; the original spelling is preserved as an alias. 2. Nutrient values sourced from USDA SR Legacy cooked lobster (moist heat) as the closest approved source; no HPB Singapore or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry for Australian rock lobster was identified. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source for cooked lobster; naturally zero as lobster is a marine crustacean with negligible trans fatty acid content. 4. sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source; naturally zero as lobster contains no free sugars. 5. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source; naturally zero as lobster is an animal product containing no dietary fibre. 6. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source for cooked lobster. 7. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 — not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry used; value set to sentinel -1 as the source does not provide this nutrient for this food item. 8. GI is an analogous estimate of 5 based on the negligible carbohydrate content (1.3 g per 100 g); no direct GI measurement for Australian rock lobster was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 9. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type as no Australia-specific rock lobster codes exist in FoodOn, FoodEx2, or SNOMED CT. 10. This record represents plain cooked lobster meat (100 g); restaurant preparations with added butter, cheese, or sauces will alter the nutritional profile significantly.
